case_title: Shri D.K. Basu,Ashok K. Johri vs State Of West Bengal,State Of U.P (1996)
case_summary: This case originated from letters to the Chief Justice of India highlighting deaths in police custody. The court treated these letters as writ petitions under public interest litigation. The petitioners sought accountability for custodial deaths and compensation for victims. The respondents, various state governments, initially denied any wrongdoing. The Supreme Court, concerned about increasing custodial violence, formulated guidelines to minimize such incidents and ensure compensation for victims' families.
legal_issue: Whether custodial violence, including torture and death in police lock-ups, violates the fundamental rights guaranteed under Articles 21 and 22 of the Constitution of India, and if so, what measures are necessary to protect these rights and provide redressal to victims.
petitioner_stand: The petitioners argued that custodial deaths and torture are rampant and violate fundamental rights. They sought the development of "custody jurisprudence," formulation of modalities for awarding compensation to victims and their families, and accountability for those responsible for custodial violence.
respondent_stand: The respondent State governments initially maintained that they were not hushing up any matters of lock-up deaths and that action was being taken against police personnel found responsible. They characterized the writ petitions as misconceived, misleading, and untenable in law.
relevant_laws: Article 5 Universal Declaration of Human Rights

case_title: L. Chandra Kumar vs Union Of India And Others (1997)
case_summary: This case involves a batch of petitions challenging the constitutional validity of Article 323A and 323B of the Constitution, which established administrative tribunals. The central issue is whether these tribunals, designed to handle specific disputes, unconstitutionally exclude the High Courts' power of judicial review under Articles 226/227 and the Supreme Court's power under Article 32. The petitioners argued that the tribunals, as structured, undermine the constitutional scheme of judicial review. The Supreme Court was asked to determine the extent of the tribunals' jurisdiction and their impact on the High Courts' powers.
legal_issue: Does the power conferred upon Parliament or State Legislatures by Article 323A(2)(d) or Article 323B(3)(d) of the Constitution to exclude the jurisdiction of all courts, except the Supreme Court under Article 136, in respect of disputes and complaints referred to in Article 323A(1) or Article 323B(2), run counter to the power of judicial review conferred on the High Courts under Articles 226/227 and on the Supreme Court under Article 32 of the Constitution?
petitioner_stand: The petitioners challenged the constitutional validity of Article 323A and 323B, arguing that excluding the jurisdiction of High Courts under Articles 226/227 and potentially the Supreme Court under Article 32 violates the basic structure of the Constitution. They sought a declaration that the tribunals' powers should not impinge on the High Courts' power of judicial review.
respondent_stand: The respondent, the Union of India, likely argued that the establishment of administrative tribunals under Articles 323A and 323B is a valid exercise of legislative power, aimed at reducing the burden on courts and providing speedy relief. They would have contended that the tribunals are an effective alternative mechanism for dispute resolution and do not unconstitutionally infringe upon the power of judicial review, especially considering the Supreme Court's jurisdiction under Article 136.

case_title: Maneka Gandhi vs Union Of India (1978)
case_summary: Maneka Gandhi's passport was impounded by the government under Section 10(3)(c) of the Passports Act, 1967, citing 'public interest.' She was denied the reasons for the impoundment. Gandhi challenged this action, arguing it violated her fundamental rights. The Supreme Court held that the right to travel abroad is part of personal liberty under Article 21, and any procedure restricting it must be fair, just, and reasonable. The court also ruled that Articles 19 and 21 are not mutually exclusive and that laws impacting personal liberty must also meet the requirements of Article 19.
legal_issue: Whether Section 10(3)(c) of the Passports Act, 1967, and the action of impounding a passport without providing reasons, violates Articles 14, 19(1)(a), and 21 of the Constitution of India.
petitioner_stand: Maneka Gandhi argued that impounding her passport without providing reasons violated her fundamental rights under Articles 14, 19(1)(a), and 21 of the Constitution. She sought the quashing of the impoundment order and the return of her passport, asserting that the right to travel abroad is a part of personal liberty and any restriction must be reasonable and fair.
respondent_stand: The Union of India contended that the impoundment was justified in 'public interest' under Section 10(3)(c) of the Passports Act, 1967. They argued that providing the reasons for impoundment would be against the 'general public interest.'

case_title: Olga Tellis & Ors vs Bombay Municipal Corporation & Ors. Etc (1985)
case_summary: This case involved pavement and slum dwellers in Bombay facing eviction by the Bombay Municipal Corporation. The dwellers argued that eviction deprived them of their livelihood, violating their right to life under Article 21 of the Constitution. The Municipal Corporation argued that the dwellers were trespassers and had no right to encroach on public land. The Supreme Court held that the right to life includes the right to livelihood, but upheld the Corporation's power to remove encroachments, subject to certain conditions and considerations for alternative housing.
legal_issue: Does the right to life under Article 21 of the Constitution of India include the right to livelihood, and if so, how does this right interact with the state's power to remove encroachments on public land?
petitioner_stand: The petitioners argued that eviction from their pavement dwellings and slum hutments would deprive them of their livelihood, violating their fundamental right to life under Article 21. They sought a declaration that the Bombay Municipal Corporation's actions were unconstitutional and an injunction against further evictions without due process.
respondent_stand: The respondents argued that the petitioners were trespassers with no legal right to encroach on public land. They contended that the Bombay Municipal Corporation Act allowed for the removal of encroachments and that the evictions were necessary for public order and safety. They also argued that the petitioners were estopped from claiming a right to livelihood because they had previously conceded they had no right to build on public land.

case_title: A.K. Gopalan vs. The State Of Madras (1950)
case_summary: A.K. Gopalan, detained under the Preventive Detention Act, 1950, petitioned the Supreme Court for a writ of habeas corpus, arguing the Act violated his fundamental rights under Articles 13, 19, 21, and 22 of the Constitution. He claimed the Act was unconstitutional and his detention illegal. The Court upheld the validity of the Preventive Detention Act, except for Section 14, which restricted disclosure of detention grounds to the court. The majority held that Article 19 doesn't apply to laws of preventive detention, and the Act didn't violate Articles 21 and 22.
legal_issue: Does the Preventive Detention Act, 1950, violate fundamental rights guaranteed under Articles 13, 19, 21, and 22 of the Constitution of India, specifically concerning personal liberty and freedom of movement?
petitioner_stand: A.K. Gopalan argued that the Preventive Detention Act, 1950, contravened Articles 13, 19, 21, and 22 of the Constitution, making it ultra vires and his detention illegal. He sought a writ of habeas corpus and his release from detention.
respondent_stand: The State of Madras and the Union of India defended the validity of the Preventive Detention Act, 1950, arguing that it was a valid law enacted by the Parliament and did not violate any fundamental rights guaranteed by the Constitution.

case_title: Janata Dal vs H.S. Chowdhary And Ors. (1992)
case_summary: This case involves allegations of bribery and corruption in the purchase of guns from the Swedish company A.B. Before by the Indian government. A First Information Report (FIR) was registered by the CBI, and a letter rogatory was issued to Switzerland for investigation. H.S. Chowdhary, claiming to be a public interest litigant, challenged the FIR and the letter rogatory. The High Court dismissed his petition for lack of locus standi, and the Supreme Court upheld that decision, finding no grounds to interfere with the investigation.
legal_issue: Does a person claiming to be a public interest litigant have locus standi to challenge the registration of a First Information Report (FIR) and the issuance of a letter rogatory in a criminal investigation, particularly when the investigation is at a preliminary stage?
petitioner_stand: The petitioner, H.S. Chowdhary, argued that the FIR and the letter rogatory should be quashed or stayed until allegations against named persons were established. He sought to intervene in the inquiry as a public interest litigant and argued that the court should determine the question of dual criminality before issuing the letter rogatory.
respondent_stand: The respondents, including the CBI, argued that the petitioner lacked locus standi to challenge the FIR and the letter rogatory. They contended that the investigation was ongoing and that the petitioner had no direct interest or special loss to justify his intervention.

case_title: A.R. Antulay vs R.S. Nayak & Anr (1988)
case_summary: A.R. Antulay, former Chief Minister of Maharashtra, was accused of corruption. R.S. Nayak filed a complaint against him. The Supreme Court initially transferred the case from a Special Judge to the High Court for expedited trial. Antulay challenged this transfer, arguing it violated his fundamental rights and the Criminal Law Amendment Act, which mandated trial by a Special Judge. The Supreme Court, in this judgment, acknowledged its error in the previous transfer order and held that it lacked the jurisdiction to transfer the case to the High Court, thus violating Antulay's rights.
legal_issue: Can the Supreme Court, in a proceeding inter partes, correct its own directions if those directions are found to be bad in law, violative of Articles 14 and 21 of the Constitution, and cause prejudice and injury to a party, even if those directions have already been issued?
petitioner_stand: A.R. Antulay argued that the Supreme Court's direction to transfer his case to the High Court violated his fundamental rights under Articles 14 and 21, as well as the provisions of the Criminal Law Amendment Act, 1952, which mandated trial by a Special Judge. He sought the quashing of all proceedings subsequent to the initial transfer order and a direction for the trial to proceed in accordance with the 1952 Act.
respondent_stand: The judgment does not explicitly detail R.S. Nayak's arguments. However, implicitly, the respondent likely argued that the Supreme Court's previous orders were binding and valid, and that the High Court had the authority to try the case as directed. The respondent likely opposed any attempt to overturn the previous orders.

case_title: Ajay Hasia Etc vs Khalid Mujib Sehravardi & Ors. Etc (1980)
case_summary: Several petitioners challenged the validity of admissions to the Regional Engineering College, Srinagar, arguing that the college, registered as a society, was acting arbitrarily in its admission process. The petitioners contended that the college should be considered a 'State' under Article 12 of the Constitution and thus subject to fundamental rights. The Supreme Court held that the college was indeed a 'State' due to the deep and pervasive control of the Central and State governments, but ultimately dismissed the writ petitions, finding some aspects of the admission process arbitrary but declining to overturn the admissions due to the advanced stage of the academic year.
legal_issue: Whether the Regional Engineering College, Srinagar, registered as a society under the Jammu & Kashmir Registration of Societies Act, 1898, is a 'State' within the meaning of Article 12 of the Constitution of India and therefore subject to the fundamental rights guaranteed under Part III of the Constitution.
petitioner_stand: The petitioners argued that the Regional Engineering College, Srinagar, should be considered a 'State' under Article 12 and that its admission procedures, particularly the high weightage given to oral interviews and the alleged arbitrariness of the interview process, violated Article 14. They sought the quashing of the admissions made for the academic year 1979-80 and a fresh admission process.
respondent_stand: The respondents likely argued that the college, as a registered society, was not a 'State' under Article 12 and therefore not subject to the same constitutional limitations. They would have defended the admission procedures as reasonable and necessary for assessing the suitability of candidates, and argued against overturning admissions already made.
relevant_laws: Jammu & Kashmir Registration of Societies Act, 1898

case_title: Central Inland Water Transport Corporation Ltd. & Anr. Etc. vs Brojo Nath Ganguly & Anr. (1986)
case_summary: Brojo Nath Ganguly and Tarun Kanti Sengupta, employees of Central Inland Water Transport Corporation (a government company), were terminated under Rule 9(i) of the Corporation's service rules, which allowed termination with three months' notice. They challenged the termination and the validity of Rule 9(i) in the Calcutta High Court. The High Court ruled in their favor, holding the Corporation to be 'the State' under Article 12 and Rule 9(i) to be ultra vires Article 14. The Supreme Court upheld the High Court's decision, finding Rule 9(i) to be unconscionable and violative of Article 14.
legal_issue: Whether Rule 9(i) of the Central Inland Water Transport Corporation Limited Service, Discipline and Appeal Rules, 1979, allowing termination of permanent employees with three months' notice, is void as being opposed to public policy under Section 23 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 and as violating Article 14 of the Constitution of India.
petitioner_stand: The Central Inland Water Transport Corporation argued that as a government company, it is not 'the State' under Article 12 and that a contract of employment entered into by it is like any other contract. They contended that a term in that contract cannot be struck down under Article 14 on the ground that it is arbitrary or unreasonable or unconscionable or one-sided or unfair. The Corporation sought to overturn the High Court's decision and uphold the validity of Rule 9(i) and the termination orders.
respondent_stand: Brojo Nath Ganguly and Tarun Kanti Sengupta argued that the Corporation is 'the State' under Article 12 and is bound to act fairly and reasonably. They contended that Rule 9(i) is an unconscionable term in a contract of employment and is therefore bad in law and can be struck down under Article 14. They sought to uphold the High Court's decision quashing the termination orders and declaring Rule 9(i) void.

case_title: Rupa Ashok Hurra vs Ashok Hurra & Anr (2002)
case_summary: This case involves a writ petition filed in the Supreme Court of India after a review petition against a previous judgment was dismissed. The central issue is whether an aggrieved person can seek relief against a final judgment of the Supreme Court after their review petition has been rejected. The petitioners argued that there should be a mechanism to correct gross injustices even after review. The court considered whether Article 32 or inherent powers could be invoked. The Supreme Court ultimately held that while Article 32 cannot be used to challenge its own final judgments, a limited remedy might exist under its inherent powers to address gross abuse of process or miscarriage of justice, subject to specific procedural safeguards.
legal_issue: Whether an aggrieved person is entitled to any relief against a final judgment/order of the Supreme Court of India, after dismissal of a review petition, either under Article 32 of the Constitution or otherwise.
petitioner_stand: The petitioner argued that the principle of finality should be relaxed in cases of jurisdictional errors, violations of natural justice or fundamental rights, or gross injustice. They sought the court's intervention under its inherent jurisdiction, suggesting a mechanism for re-examination of cases certified by a senior counsel.
respondent_stand: The respondents, including the Attorney-General appearing as amicus curiae, conceded that Article 32 cannot be invoked to challenge a final judgment after review. However, they advocated for a limited opportunity to seek relief under the court's inherent powers in cases of gross abuse of process or miscarriage of justice, subject to procedural safeguards to prevent frivolous applications.

case_title: Yatin Narendra Oza vs Suo Motu, High Court Of Gujarat (2026)
case_summary: This case involves Yatin Narendra Oza, President of the Gujarat High Court Advocates’ Association, who was found guilty of criminal contempt by the Gujarat High Court for making scandalous allegations against the High Court and its Registry during a press conference. The High Court sentenced him and also recalled his senior advocate designation. Oza appealed to the Supreme Court, challenging his conviction and the High Court's actions. The Supreme Court had to determine the validity of the contempt conviction and the High Court's refusal to accept Oza's apology.
legal_issue: Whether the High Court erred in convicting the appellant for criminal contempt under Section 2(c)(i) of the Contempt of Courts Act, 1971, and refusing to accept his apology, considering his allegations against the High Court and its Registry during a press conference?
petitioner_stand: Yatin Narendra Oza, the appellant, argued that his statements were made in the context of voicing the grievances of junior advocates during the COVID-19 pandemic and that he tendered an unconditional apology to the High Court. He sought relief from the contempt conviction and the restoration of his senior advocate designation.
respondent_stand: The High Court of Gujarat, acting suo motu, argued that Oza's allegations were unfounded, unwarranted, and scandalous, undermining the credibility of the High Court and its administration. They contended that his apology was insincere and a calculated strategy to avoid the consequences of his actions, justifying the contempt conviction and the revocation of his senior advocate designation.

case_title: Vepanjeri Dileep Kumar vs State Of Odisha (2026)
case_summary: Vepanjeri Dileep Kumar sought bail from the Orissa High Court after his bail application was rejected in a case involving offences under Sections 370/370(3)/376-D/294/323/506 of the BNSS. He was arrested in Andhra Pradesh and brought to Odisha. The petitioner argued that his arrest and detention were illegal because he was not produced before a magistrate within 24 hours of his arrest, violating Section 58 of the BNSS and Article 22(2) of the Constitution of India. The High Court considered the legality of the arrest and detention, focusing on the mandatory requirement of producing an arrestee before a magistrate within 24 hours.
legal_issue: Whether the failure to produce an arrestee before the nearest magistrate within 24 hours of arrest, as mandated by Section 58 of the BNSS and Article 22(2) of the Constitution of India, renders the arrest and subsequent detention illegal, even if the arrest occurs outside the local jurisdiction of the court where the case is registered.
petitioner_stand: The petitioner argued that his arrest and detention were illegal because he was apprehended in Andhra Pradesh but not produced before any magistrate within 24 hours of his apprehension. He sought bail based on the violation of his fundamental right under Article 22(2) of the Constitution and the statutory requirement of Section 58 of the BNSS.
respondent_stand: The State of Odisha, represented by the Additional Public Prosecutor, argued that the police followed proper procedure, although the specifics of their defense regarding the 24-hour rule are not fully detailed in this excerpt. The State submitted an affidavit from the IIC Raighar Police Station explaining the circumstances of the arrest and the reasons for the delay in producing the petitioner before a magistrate.

case_title: Urmila Devi vs The State Of Uttar Pradesh (2026)
case_summary: Urmila Devi and Manoj Devi contested a Pradhan election in Uttar Pradesh. Urmila Devi filed an election petition alleging irregularities in the vote counting, which the Sub Divisional Officer (SDO) allowed, ordering a recount. Manoj Devi challenged this order in the High Court, which set aside the SDO's order, arguing the SDO became functus officio after ordering the recount. Urmila Devi appealed to the Supreme Court. The Supreme Court upheld the High Court's decision, finding that the SDO lost jurisdiction after issuing the initial order for recounting.
legal_issue: Does a Prescribed Authority, under Section 12-C of the Uttar Pradesh Panchayat Raj Act, 1947, become functus officio and lose jurisdiction to pass further orders related to an election petition after issuing an order for recounting of votes?
petitioner_stand: Urmila Devi argued that the High Court erred in interfering with the SDO's order for recounting, as there were significant discrepancies in the initial vote count. She contended that the recounting, which declared her the winner, proved the irregularities materially affected the election result and that the SDO had the authority to order the recount.
respondent_stand: Manoj Devi argued that the High Court correctly set aside the SDO's order because the SDO became functus officio after allowing the election petition and ordering a recount. She argued that recounting is only permissible with specific allegations of illegal vote counting, which were absent in this case, and that the statutory scheme does not allow for recounting as an independent relief after the declaration of results.
relevant_laws: Section 12C Uttar Pradesh Panchayat Raj Act, 1947
Rule 4(3) U.P. Panchayat Raj (Settlement of Election Disputes) Rules, 1994
Rules 104, 105 and 106 of the U.P. Panchayat Election Rules, 1994

case_title: Kamlesh Mehta vs Table Tennis Federation Of India And Ors (2026)
case_summary: Kamlesh Mehta, Secretary General of the Table Tennis Federation of India (TTFI), filed a petition challenging an order by the TTFI's Executive Committee that declared him persona non grata and suspended him pending an inquiry. Mehta argued the order violated natural justice principles. The TTFI defended the suspension citing emergent circumstances and its MoA. The Delhi High Court considered whether the petition was maintainable and whether the suspension order violated principles of natural justice. The Court ultimately ruled in favor of the petitioner, finding the suspension order unsustainable.
legal_issue: Whether the suspension of the Secretary General of the Table Tennis Federation of India by its Executive Committee, without affording him any opportunity of hearing, violates the principles of natural justice.
petitioner_stand: Kamlesh Mehta argued that the suspension order and the relevant clause in the TTFI's Memorandum of Association (MoA) allowing suspension without adherence to natural justice were illegal. He sought the quashing of the suspension order and argued that the Enquiry Committee was biased due to its members being part of the Executive Committee.
respondent_stand: The Table Tennis Federation of India (TTFI) argued that the petition was not maintainable due to disputed facts and the availability of arbitration. They contended the suspension was justified by emergent circumstances and that Clause 11(d) of the MoA allowed bypassing natural justice principles.

case_title: Khushnuma Khan & Ors vs Union Of India Through Its Secretary ... (2026)
case_summary: This case involves residents of three camps ('Bhai Ram Camp', 'DID Camp', and 'Masjid Camp') in Delhi, who were contesting their eviction and relocation to alternate accommodation in Savda Ghevra by the Union of India. The petitioners argued that the relocation violated their fundamental rights, particularly the right to livelihood and adequate housing under Article 21 of the Constitution, and that the relocation process did not comply with applicable government policies and Supreme Court guidelines. The Delhi High Court considered whether the relocation violated the petitioners' fundamental rights and contravened existing laws and government policies. The court ultimately ruled that the relocation must ensure minimal impact on the lives of those being relocated, and that the respondents are bound by their own policies to ensure basic amenities are available at the relocation site.
legal_issue: Does the relocation of residents from 'jhuggi jhopri bastis' to alternate accommodation, undertaken by the Union of India, violate the residents' fundamental right to life under Article 21 of the Constitution of India, particularly concerning their right to livelihood and adequate housing, when the relocation process and the alternate accommodation may lack essential amenities and negatively impact their existing means of sustenance?
petitioner_stand: The petitioners argued that the relocation violates their right to life under Article 21, as it disrupts their livelihoods and access to essential services like education and healthcare. They sought to set aside the eviction notices, arguing that the relocation site is too far from their current workplaces and schools, and that the respondents failed to comply with procedural safeguards and applicable policies regarding rehabilitation.
respondent_stand: The respondents argued that the petitioners were deemed eligible for rehabilitation at the alternate accommodation, rendering the petitions infructuous. They claimed that the relocation decision was approved by the High Powered Committee and DUSIB, and that the alternate accommodation has basic amenities. They also stated that the eviction was necessary for strategic reasons and to strengthen defense infrastructure.
relevant_laws: Bombay Municipal Corporation Act, 1888 Sections 312(1), 313(1)(a) and 314
Delhi Urban Shelter Improvement Board Act, 2010 (DUSIB Act)
Delhi Slum and JJ Rehabilitation Policy, 2015

case_title: Sterlite Technologies Ltd vs Bharat Sanchar Nigam Limited (2026)
case_summary: Sterlite Technologies Ltd. (Sterlite) and Bharat Sanchar Nigam Limited (BSNL) were in dispute over payment for laying optical fiber cables. Sterlite was contracted to lay cables for BSNL, and the dispute arose regarding 547 kms of cables laid in a common trench with multiple ducts. Sterlite claimed 100% service cost for these cables, while BSNL argued that only 25% was payable as per a pre-bid clarification for cables laid through existing ducts. The arbitrator rejected Sterlite's claim, and Sterlite challenged the arbitral award in the Delhi High Court, which upheld the arbitrator's decision.
legal_issue: Whether the arbitral tribunal's interpretation of the contract, specifically regarding the definition of "existing ducts" and the applicability of a pre-bid clarification on payment for cables laid in multiple duct trenches, was perverse and warranted interference under Section 34 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996.
petitioner_stand: Sterlite argued that the arbitrator misinterpreted the contract by applying a pre-bid clarification intended for government-owned ducts to their situation, where they laid multiple ducts in a single trench. They sought the full service cost for the cables laid, contending that the 25% payment rate was inapplicable.
respondent_stand: BSNL argued that the arbitrator's interpretation was plausible and that the 25% payment rate applied because Sterlite was using 'existing ducts' when laying subsequent cables in the same trench. They emphasized that the contract included multiple ducting from the beginning and that other bidders accepted the 25% payment for similar work.
relevant_laws: Section 34 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996
Section 21 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996
Section 28(3) of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996

case_title: Sh. Mohan Lal Sharma vs The State (2026)
case_summary: Mohan Lal Sharma, son of the deceased Smt. Shanti Devi, petitioned the Delhi District Court seeking probate of her will. Some of the deceased's other legal heirs (respondents 6-10) contested the will, alleging it was fabricated and that the deceased lacked testamentary capacity. The court considered the evidence, including the registered will and testimony of an attesting witness. The court found the will to be genuine and duly executed. The court granted Letters of Administration, as no executor was named in the will, to Mohan Lal Sharma.
legal_issue: Whether the petitioner is entitled to Letters of Administration in respect of the will of the deceased, Smt. Shanti Devi, given the objections raised regarding the will's genuineness and the testator's capacity?
petitioner_stand: Mohan Lal Sharma, the petitioner, argued that the will was validly executed by his mother, Smt. Shanti Devi, bequeathing the subject property to him. He sought Letters of Administration to administer the estate according to the will's terms.
respondent_stand: Respondents 6-10 contested the petition, arguing that the will was fabricated, the deceased lacked testamentary capacity due to illness and age, and that the will was registered in Uttar Pradesh despite the deceased residing in Delhi. They also argued that a pending suit for possession barred the probate proceedings.
relevant_laws: Section 276 of the Indian Succession Act, 1925
Section 10 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908
Section 114 of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872
Section 63 of the Indian Succession Act, 1925
Section 67 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam
Schedule VII of the Indian Succession Act, 1925
Section 291 of the Indian Succession Act, 1925
Section 317 of the Indian Succession Act, 1925

case_title: R.K Transport Company vs M/S/ Bharat Aluminum Company Limited (2026)
case_summary: R.K. Transport Company (RKTC) appealed a decision by the Commercial Court that set aside an arbitral award in their favor against Bharat Aluminum Company Limited (BALCO). RKTC and BALCO had a contract for mining and transporting alumina bauxite, which led to disputes over payments. The arbitrator initially ruled in favor of RKTC, but the Commercial Court overturned this decision. The central dispute revolves around whether the Commercial Court overstepped its bounds by re-evaluating evidence and re-interpreting the contract. The High Court is reviewing whether the Commercial Court erred in setting aside the arbitral award.
legal_issue: Did the Commercial Court exceed its jurisdiction under Section 34 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act by re-appreciating evidence and re-interpreting the contract when setting aside the arbitral award?
petitioner_stand: RKTC argued that the Commercial Court exceeded its jurisdiction by re-appreciating evidence and re-interpreting the contract, which is not permissible under Section 34 of the Arbitration Act. They sought the restoration of the original arbitral award, including the awarded amount and interest.
respondent_stand: BALCO contended that the Commercial Court correctly set aside the arbitral award after properly appreciating the evidence. They argued that the arbitrator's decision was flawed due to a lack of evidence supporting RKTC's claims and that certain claims were barred by contractual provisions and public policy. They sought dismissal of the appeal.

case_title: M/s.Sri City Pvt. Ltd. v. N.Sakkubayamma (died) per LRs and others, Andhra Pradesh High Court, 2026
case_summary: This case involves multiple writ appeals and a writ petition concerning land originally assigned to displaced persons from Sriharikota. The State of Andhra Pradesh sought to resume the assigned lands for establishing a Special Economic Zone (SEZ) and industrial estate. The original writ petitioners challenged the resumption order, arguing that it was illegal. The High Court is reviewing the legality of the land resumption process and the compensation offered to the displaced persons.
legal_issue: Whether the State government can resume assigned lands, granted to displaced persons for rehabilitation, for public purposes such as establishing a Special Economic Zone, and if so, what is the appropriate compensation or ex-gratia payment due to the assignees.
petitioner_stand: The petitioners (original land assignees) argued that the resumption of their assigned lands was illegal and sought a direction for the State authorities to initiate acquisition proceedings under the Land Acquisition Act, 1894, if the land was required for public purposes. They claimed entitlement to fair compensation for the loss of their land and livelihood.
respondent_stand: The respondents (State of Andhra Pradesh, APIIC, and M/s. Sri City Pvt. Ltd.) contended that the lands were assigned and not owned by the petitioners, and that the State had the right to resume the lands for public purposes. They argued that the petitioners were entitled to ex-gratia payments as per existing government orders, but not full compensation under the Land Acquisition Act.
relevant_laws: G.O.Ms.No.1024, Industries and Commerce Department dated 02.11.1970

case_title: Vishaka & Ors vs State Of Rajasthan & Ors (1997)
case_summary: This case was brought as a public interest litigation by Vishaka and other social activists against the State of Rajasthan, following the gang rape of a social worker. The petitioners sought to address the lack of legal protection against sexual harassment for working women in India. The Supreme Court, recognizing the violation of fundamental rights under Articles 14, 19, and 21, issued guidelines and norms to prevent sexual harassment at workplaces. These guidelines were to be treated as law until the legislature enacted specific legislation on the matter.
legal_issue: In the absence of domestic legislation, can the Supreme Court issue guidelines to address sexual harassment in the workplace, relying on fundamental rights and international conventions, and treat these guidelines as law under Article 141 of the Constitution until the legislature enacts specific legislation?
petitioner_stand: The petitioners argued that the fundamental rights of working women under Articles 14, 19, and 21 were being violated due to the lack of legal protection against sexual harassment at workplaces. They sought the Court's intervention to provide guidelines and mechanisms to prevent and address sexual harassment, ensuring a safe working environment for women.
respondent_stand: The State of Rajasthan and the Union of India acknowledged the problem of sexual harassment and assisted the Court in formulating appropriate guidelines. The Union of India, through the Solicitor General, consented to the guidelines and norms proposed by the Court.
relevant_laws: Section 2(d) Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993

case_title: Justice K.S.Puttaswamy(Retd) And Anr. vs Union Of India And Ors. (2017)
case_summary: This case involved a batch of writ petitions challenging the constitutional validity of the Aadhaar scheme, primarily on the grounds of violation of the right to privacy. The Attorney General argued that previous Supreme Court decisions in *M.P. Sharma* and *Kharak Singh* cast doubt on the existence of a fundamental right to privacy. The petitioners contended that these earlier decisions were based on principles no longer valid and that subsequent judgments affirmed the right to privacy. The court constituted a nine-judge bench to determine whether privacy is a constitutionally protected right under the Indian Constitution.
legal_issue: Whether the right to privacy is a constitutionally protected fundamental right under the Indian Constitution.
petitioner_stand: The petitioners argued that the right to privacy is an integral part of personal liberty under Article 21 and other fundamental rights. They sought a declaration that the Aadhaar scheme violates this right to privacy and is therefore unconstitutional.
respondent_stand: The Union of India argued that there is no explicit fundamental right to privacy under the Indian Constitution, relying on the decisions in *M.P. Sharma* and *Kharak Singh*. They contended that the state has the power to regulate activities for social security, even if it involves some intrusion into privacy.
relevant_laws: Regulation 228 of Chapter XX of the U P Police Regulations
Regulation 236 of Chapter XX of the U P Police Regulations

case_title: Navtej Singh Johar vs Union Of India Ministry Of Law And ... (2018)
case_summary: This case involved multiple petitioners challenging the constitutional validity of Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC), which criminalized "carnal intercourse against the order of nature." The petitioners argued that Section 377 violated fundamental rights, including the right to privacy, equality, and dignity. The Union of India defended the law, citing social morality. The Supreme Court ultimately decriminalized consensual homosexual acts between adults, holding Section 377 unconstitutional to the extent that it criminalized such acts.
legal_issue: Whether Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code, insofar as it criminalizes consensual sexual acts between adults of the same sex, violates fundamental rights guaranteed under Articles 14, 15, 19, and 21 of the Constitution of India.
petitioner_stand: The petitioners argued that Section 377 violated the rights to sexuality, sexual autonomy, and choice of sexual partner, which are integral to the right to life under Article 21. They sought a declaration that Section 377 was unconstitutional and that the LGBT community's rights should be recognized and protected.
respondent_stand: The Union of India initially defended Section 377, arguing that it was necessary to maintain social order and morality. Some intervenors argued that decriminalizing homosexual acts would be against the order of nature and could have negative social consequences.

case_title: Dr. Jaya Thakur vs Government Of India (2026)
case_summary: Dr. Jaya Thakur, a social worker, filed a public interest litigation against the Government of India and various states, seeking directions to provide free sanitary pads to female students in classes 6-12 and separate toilets for females in government-aided and residential schools. The petitioner argued that the lack of these provisions violates fundamental rights. The respondents acknowledged the issues and outlined existing schemes and policies. The court considered whether the lack of menstrual hygiene facilities violates constitutional rights to equality, dignity, and education. The court ultimately issued directions to ensure proper implementation of existing schemes and policies.
legal_issue: Does the unavailability of gender-segregated toilets and non-access to menstrual absorbents violate the rights to equality (Article 14), dignified menstrual health (Article 21), and education (Article 21A) under the Constitution of India, as well as the right to free and compulsory education under the Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009?
petitioner_stand: The petitioner argued that the lack of free sanitary pads and separate toilets in schools violates the fundamental rights of girl students, leading to absenteeism and school dropouts. The petitioner sought a writ of mandamus directing the respondents to provide these facilities and implement awareness programs on menstrual health.
respondent_stand: The respondents acknowledged the taboos and socio-cultural restrictions surrounding menstruation and the limited access to sanitary products and safe facilities. They presented existing schemes and policies aimed at addressing these issues, including providing sanitary napkins at subsidized rates, installing vending machines and incinerators in schools, and promoting awareness programs.
relevant_laws: Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009
Section 3 Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009
Section 19 Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009
Article 26 Universal Declaration of Human Rights
Article 13 International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights

case_title: Harish Rana vs Union Of India (2026)
case_summary: Harish Rana, in a persistent vegetative state (PVS) for 13 years following an accident, sought through his parents to discontinue clinically assisted nutrition and hydration (CANH). The High Court initially dismissed the plea, but the Supreme Court, after ordering evaluations by primary and secondary medical boards, considered whether CANH constituted medical treatment and if its continuation was in Rana's best interest. The Court ultimately streamlined existing guidelines for passive euthanasia, acknowledging the family's plea to end Rana's suffering, and considered the need for legislative action on the matter.
legal_issue: Whether the administration of clinically assisted nutrition and hydration (CANH) to a patient in a persistent vegetative state constitutes 'medical treatment' that can be withdrawn or withheld in accordance with Article 21 of the Constitution of India, and if so, under what conditions and procedural safeguards.
petitioner_stand: Harish Rana, through his parents, argued that the continued administration of CANH through a PEG tube was medical treatment that was no longer beneficial and violated his right to live with dignity under Article 21. They sought a declaration that CANH is medical treatment and its suitability for continuation be assessed under the Common Cause Guidelines, ultimately seeking its withdrawal.
respondent_stand: The Union of India, while not explicitly opposing the petitioner's plea after medical evaluations, ensured due process was followed, including the constitution of medical boards and consideration of the family's wishes. The ASG conveyed the doctors' opinion that continuing medical treatment was not in the best interest of the applicant and that nature should be allowed to take its course.

case_title: Smt. Chandravva Hanamant Gokavi W/O ... vs State Of Karnataka (2026)
case_summary: Smt. Chandravva Hanamant Gokavi, a hotel worker, filed a writ petition seeking the implementation of the Karnataka government's Menstrual Leave Policy across all establishments in Belagavi district, including hotels and small commercial units. She argued that women in the unorganized sector face harsh working conditions, especially during menstruation. The State acknowledged the policy's intent but cited practical challenges in its enforcement. The court considered the historical context of menstrual leave policies and the ongoing legislative efforts in this regard.
legal_issue: Whether the State of Karnataka is obligated to implement and enforce its Menstrual Leave Policy, particularly in the unorganized labor sector, to ensure workplace dignity and gender equity under Articles 226 and 227 of the Constitution of India.
petitioner_stand: The petitioner argued that the State government should implement and enforce the Menstrual Leave Policy across all establishments, especially in the unorganized sector, to ensure gender equity and workplace dignity. She sought a writ of mandamus directing the respondents to consider her representation and frame guidelines for uniform implementation of the policy.
respondent_stand: The State acknowledged the progressive intent of the Menstrual Leave Policy but highlighted practical difficulties in monitoring and enforcing it, particularly in the unorganized sector. The State also pointed to the ongoing legislative consideration of the issue and submitted that the State is within its constitutional authority to formulate such a policy.
relevant_laws: Articles 226 and 227 of the Constitution of India
Article 32 of the Constitution of India
The Karnataka Shops and Commercial Establishments Act, 1961
The Beedi and Cigar Workers (Conditions of Employment) Act, 1966
Section 5 of the Karnataka Menstrual Leave and Hygiene Bill, 2025

case_title: L.I.C. Of India & Anr vs Consumer Education & Research Centre & Ors.ETC. (1995)
case_summary: The Life Insurance Corporation of India (LIC) appealed a Gujarat High Court decision regarding its Table 58 policy, a convertible term insurance plan. The Consumer Education & Research Centre challenged the LIC's conditions for eligibility, arguing they were arbitrary and discriminatory, violating Articles 14, 19(1)(g), and 21 of the Constitution. The High Court struck down a condition limiting eligibility to those in government, quasi-government, or reputed commercial firms. The Supreme Court considered whether the LIC could restrict the policy to a specific class of individuals.
legal_issue: Whether the Life Insurance Corporation of India can restrict its convertible term insurance policy (Table 58) to salaried persons in government, quasi-government, or reputed commercial firms without violating Articles 14, 19(1)(g), and 21 of the Constitution of India.
petitioner_stand: The LIC argued that the High Court erred in interfering with commercial contracts based on actuarial considerations. They contended that the policy conditions were necessary for the successful operation of the plan and that there was no invidious discrimination.
respondent_stand: The respondents argued that the LIC's conditions were unconstitutional and impermissible, violating fundamental rights to livelihood and social justice enshrined in Articles 21, 38, 39, 41, and 47 of the Constitution, as well as international human rights declarations.
relevant_laws: Article 25 Universal Declaration of Human Rights
Article 7 International Covenant on Economic and Social Rights

case_title: Charan Lal Sahu Etc. Etc vs Union Of India And Ors (1989)
case_summary: This case involved a challenge to the constitutional validity of the Bhopal Gas Leak Disaster (Processing of Claims) Act, 1985. The Act was enacted to allow the Indian government to represent the victims of the Bhopal gas tragedy in legal proceedings against Union Carbide. Petitioners argued the Act violated fundamental rights and principles of natural justice. The Supreme Court upheld the Act's validity, finding it a reasonable measure to protect the victims' interests, but emphasized the need for adequate notice to victims before any settlement.
legal_issue: Whether the Bhopal Gas Leak Disaster (Processing of Claims) Act, 1985, which authorized the Central Government to represent victims of the Bhopal gas tragedy, is constitutionally valid, particularly in light of Articles 14, 19, and 21 of the Constitution of India and principles of natural justice.
petitioner_stand: The petitioners argued that the Act violated fundamental rights under Articles 14, 19, and 21 of the Constitution. They contended that the Union of India, being a potential joint tort-feasor and having a conflict of interest due to its shareholding in UCIL, could not fairly represent the victims. They sought the Act to be declared unconstitutional.
respondent_stand: The Union of India argued that the Act was necessary to protect the interests of the victims, who were in a vulnerable position against a multinational corporation. They asserted the Act was a valid exercise of the state's parens patriae jurisdiction and sovereign power to ensure justice and compensation for the victims of the disaster.

Section 3 Bhopal Gas Leak Disaster (Processing of claims) Act, 1985

case_title: Gyanesh Rai And Another vs State Of U.P. And 9 Ors (2015)
case_summary: Gyanesh Rai and his father filed a writ petition alleging custodial violence by police officers. Gyanesh Rai claimed he was illegally detained and tortured at a police station, including being subjected to electric shocks and having aluminum wires inserted into his body. He sought compensation for physical and mental suffering and action against the responsible police personnel. The Allahabad High Court addressed the issue of custodial violence and the violation of fundamental rights, emphasizing the impermissibility of torture and the need for accountability of police officers.
legal_issue: Whether the alleged custodial torture of the petitioner by police officials violated Article 21 of the Constitution of India, thereby entitling the petitioner to compensation and the initiation of criminal action against the erring police personnel?
petitioner_stand: The petitioners argued that Gyanesh Rai was subjected to brutal custodial violence, including torture and illegal confinement, by the respondent police officials. They sought compensation for the physical and mental suffering caused by the torture and requested the court to direct the lodging of an FIR against the erring police personnel.
respondent_stand: The respondent, the State of Uttar Pradesh, argued that there was no excess on the part of the police personnel, asserting that their actions were motivated by the need to solve a serious crime of loot and murder. They implied that the police were acting within their duties to investigate the crime.
relevant_laws: Article 5 Universal Declaration of Human Rights

case_title: Mumtaz Ahmed Nasir Khan vs The State Of Maharashtra (2019)
case_summary: This case involves two juveniles accused of the murder of a three-and-a-half-year-old child. The Juvenile Justice Board decided that the older juvenile should be tried as an adult, while the younger one should be tried as a juvenile. The Sessions Court upheld these decisions. The victim's father appealed the decision regarding the younger juvenile, and the older juvenile filed a writ petition challenging his adult trial. The Bombay High Court had to determine the legality and propriety of the lower courts' decisions regarding the juveniles' trial status.
legal_issue: Whether the Juvenile Justice Board and Sessions Court erred in their assessment of the juveniles' mental and physical capacity, understanding of the crime's consequences, and the circumstances surrounding the alleged heinous offense, thereby incorrectly determining whether they should be tried as adults or juveniles under the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015.
petitioner_stand: The victim's father argued that both juveniles played an equal role in the gruesome murder and should be tried as adults due to the depravity of the crime and their near-adulthood. The older juvenile argued that there was no significant difference between him and the younger juvenile, and he should also be tried as a juvenile, emphasizing his normal upbringing and educational achievements.
respondent_stand: The younger juvenile argued that his involvement only began after the murder was committed by the older juvenile, and his actions did not constitute a heinous crime warranting an adult trial. The State argued in favor of the JJB and Sessions Court decisions.

case_title: Ranjanben vs State (2010)
case_summary: Ranjanben, the petitioner, sought compensation for the death of her husband, Rameshbhai Parmar, who died in police custody after being arrested for offences under Sections 323, 504, and 506(2) of the IPC. She alleged police brutality and negligence in providing medical treatment. The State of Gujarat argued that Rameshbhai's death was due to natural causes, specifically excessive alcohol consumption leading to intestinal perforation, and denied any custodial violence or negligence. The Gujarat High Court had to decide whether the state was liable to pay compensation for the death in custody.
legal_issue: Whether the State is liable to pay compensation for a death occurring in police custody, either due to custodial violence/negligence or failure to provide prompt medical treatment, thereby violating the deceased's right to life under Article 21 of the Constitution of India.
petitioner_stand: Ranjanben argued that her husband's death was a result of police brutality and negligence, as he was not provided prompt medical treatment while in custody. She sought a detailed investigation and compensation from the State of Gujarat, citing violation of her husband's fundamental rights.
respondent_stand: The State of Gujarat contended that Rameshbhai's death was due to natural causes, specifically excessive alcohol consumption, and not due to any custodial violence or negligence on the part of the police. They argued that no compensation was warranted as there was no police atrocity or negligence.

case_title: Mohamed Huzaifa Javed Ahmed Ansari vs The State Of Maharashtra (2019)
case_summary: This case involves two juveniles accused of the murder of a three-and-a-half-year-old child. The Juvenile Justice Board decided that the older juvenile should be tried as an adult, while the younger one should be tried as a juvenile. The victim's father appealed the decision regarding the younger juvenile, and the older juvenile filed a writ petition challenging the decision against him. The Bombay High Court had to determine whether the JJB and Sessions Court correctly assessed the juveniles' mental and physical capacity to commit the crime and understand its consequences, and whether the decisions to try them differently were justified.
legal_issue: Whether the Juvenile Justice Board and Sessions Court erred in their assessment under Section 15 of the Juvenile Justice Act, 2015, regarding the mental and physical capacity of two juveniles accused of a heinous crime, leading to disparate decisions on whether they should be tried as adults or juveniles.
petitioner_stand: The victim's father argued that both juveniles played an equal role in the gruesome murder and should be tried as adults, emphasizing the depravity of the crime and their near-adulthood. The older juvenile argued that there was nothing to distinguish him from the younger juvenile, and that he should also be tried as a juvenile, citing his normal upbringing and educational achievements.
respondent_stand: The younger juvenile argued that his involvement only commenced after the murder was committed by the older juvenile, and that his actions did not constitute a 'heinous crime' as defined under the Juvenile Justice Act. The State argued in favor of the JJB and Sessions Court decisions.

case_title: Ex-Capt. Harish Uppal vs Union Of India & Anr (2002)
case_summary: Ex-Capt. Harish Uppal filed a writ petition against the Union of India concerning the legality of lawyers' strikes and boycotts of courts. The petitioner sought a declaration that such actions are illegal. The Supreme Court, after hearing arguments from various Bar Associations and Councils, addressed the question of whether lawyers have a right to strike, considering their role as officers of the court and the impact on the administration of justice. The court ultimately held that lawyers do not have a right to strike or give calls for boycotts.
legal_issue: Whether lawyers have a right to strike and/or give a call for boycotts of courts, considering their role as officers of the court and the impact on the administration of justice.
petitioner_stand: The petitioner argued that strikes by lawyers are illegal and that the Bar Council of India and State Bar Councils should monitor and prevent such strikes and boycotts. They sought a declaration that strikes and calls for boycotts are illegal and that the Bar Councils should be held responsible for preventing them.
respondent_stand: The Bar Councils and Associations generally submitted that they were not in favor of strikes and/or calls for strikes, acknowledging that lawyers should not resort to strikes when legal remedies are available. The Attorney General argued that lawyers, as officers of the court, have a duty to assist in the administration of justice, making strikes inconsistent with their calling, and that abstention from work should only occur in the rarest of rare cases.

case_title: Unni Krishnan, J.P. And Ors. Etc. Etc vs State Of Andhra Pradesh And Ors. Etc. Etc (1993)
case_summary: This case involved a challenge by private educational institutions to the decision in Mohini Jain v. State of Karnataka, which held that every citizen has a right to education and that charging capitation fees by private institutions is a denial of this right. The petitioners argued for the right to establish and administer educational institutions, including the right to charge fees to cover costs. The respondents, including the State governments, argued that education is a public duty and commercialization should be prevented. The Supreme Court held that the right to education is a fundamental right up to the age of 14, subject to the State's economic capacity, and framed a scheme to regulate admissions and fees in private educational institutions.
legal_issue: Whether the right to education is a fundamental right under the Constitution of India, particularly within the context of Articles 21, 45, and 41, and if so, to what extent does this right limit the autonomy of private educational institutions in setting fees and admission policies?
petitioner_stand: The petitioners, private educational institutions, argued that they have a fundamental right under Article 19(1)(g) to establish and administer educational institutions, including the right to charge fees sufficient to cover their costs. They sought the freedom to operate without undue state interference, including the ability to set their own fee structures and admission criteria, subject only to reasonable restrictions under Article 19(6).
respondent_stand: The respondents, primarily the State governments and educational councils, argued that education is a fundamental duty of the State, which can be fulfilled through both public and private institutions. They contended that private institutions performing this public function should not commercialize education and must adhere to fair admission practices based on merit, with fees regulated to prevent exploitation.

case_title: Society For Un-Aided P.School Of Raj vs U.O.I & Anr (2012)
case_summary: The Society for Un-aided Private Schools of Rajasthan challenged the constitutional validity of the Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009, arguing it infringed on their right to establish and administer educational institutions. The Supreme Court upheld the validity of the Act, specifically Section 12(1)(c), which mandates unaided non-minority schools to reserve 25% of seats for children from weaker sections and disadvantaged groups. The court reasoned that this provision constitutes a reasonable restriction in the interest of the general public and aligns with the State's obligation to provide free and compulsory education under Article 21A of the Constitution. The court balanced the right to education with the right to establish and administer educational institutions.
legal_issue: Does Section 12(1)(c) of the Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009, which mandates unaided non-minority schools to reserve 25% of seats for children from weaker sections and disadvantaged groups, constitute a reasonable restriction on the right to establish and administer educational institutions under Article 19(1)(g) of the Constitution, as saved by Article 19(6)?
petitioner_stand: The petitioner, Society for Un-aided Private Schools of Rajasthan, argued that the Right to Education Act, particularly Section 12(1)(c), infringes upon their fundamental right under Article 19(1)(g) to establish and administer educational institutions. They sought a declaration that the Act is unconstitutional and inapplicable to unaided non-minority schools.
respondent_stand: The respondent, Union of India, argued that the Right to Education Act is constitutionally valid and necessary to fulfill the State's obligation under Article 21A to provide free and compulsory education to all children. They contended that Section 12(1)(c) is a reasonable restriction on the right under Article 19(1)(g), justified in the interest of the general public and to promote social justice.

case_title: Maharshi Mahesh Jogi vs State Of M.P. & Ors (2013)
case_summary: Maharshi Mahesh Yogi Vedic Vishwavidyalaya appealed against a Madhya Pradesh High Court decision that partially upheld amendments to the Maharshi Mahesh Yogi Vedic Vishwavidyalaya Adhiniyam, 1995. The amendments, introduced in 2000, altered the powers and functions of the University. The University challenged the amendments, arguing they restricted its scope of education beyond Vedic studies. The Supreme Court examined the validity of the amendments in light of the University's objectives and the importance of education, ultimately ruling on which amendments were valid and which were not.
legal_issue: Whether the amendments introduced to Sections 2, 4, 9 and 17, as well as insertion of Sections 31-A, 31-B, 31-C, 37-A, 37-B to the Maharshi Mahesh Yogi Vedic Vishwavidyalaya Adhiniyam, 1995 (Act No.37 of 1995) by Amendment Act No.5 of 2000, unconstitutionally infringe upon the autonomy and objectives of the University.
petitioner_stand: The University argued that the amendments, particularly those restricting the scope of education to solely Vedic studies, crippled its activities and did injustice to its beneficiaries. They sought the amendments to be declared ultra vires, allowing the University to continue offering a broader range of educational programs.
respondent_stand: The State of Madhya Pradesh likely argued that the amendments were necessary to regulate the University's activities and ensure it adhered to its original mandate of promoting Vedic learning. They likely defended the amendments as being within the state's legislative competence and not infringing upon any fundamental rights.
relevant_laws: Maharshi Mahesh Yogi Vedic Vishwavidyalaya Adhiniyam, 1995 (Act No.37 of 1995)
Article 26 Universal Declaration of Human Rights

case_title: Miss Mohini Jain vs State Of Karnataka And Ors (1992)
case_summary: Miss Mohini Jain challenged a Karnataka government notification allowing private medical colleges to charge exorbitant fees from students not admitted through government seats. She was denied admission to an MBBS course because she couldn't afford the tuition. The Supreme Court addressed whether a 'right to education' exists under the Constitution and whether charging capitation fees violates it. The Court held that the right to education is a fundamental right flowing from the right to life and that charging capitation fees is unconstitutional and violates Article 14.
legal_issue: Whether the charging of capitation fees by private educational institutions for admission violates the right to education and the equality clause under Article 14 of the Constitution of India.
petitioner_stand: Miss Mohini Jain argued that the notification allowing private medical colleges to charge exorbitant fees was unconstitutional. She sought admission to the MBBS course without having to pay the high tuition fee, asserting her right to education.
respondent_stand: The State of Karnataka and the private medical colleges argued that charging higher fees from non-meritorious students (those not admitted through government seats) was justified to cover expenses. They contended that there was no constitutional or legal provision forbidding capitation fees, and that the fees were not excessive.
relevant_laws: Section 3 Karnataka Educational Institutions (Prohibition of Capitation Fee) Act, 1984
Section 5(1) Karnataka Educational Institutions (Prohibition of Capitation Fee) Act, 1984

case_title: Smt. Ranjanben Rambhai Patel vs State Of Gujarat (2000)
case_summary: This case involves a petition filed by a union of pre-primary teachers whose schools (Balmandirs) were located in villages merged into the Ahmedabad Municipal Corporation.  The teachers sought to be absorbed as employees of the Corporation and for the Balmandirs to continue operating. The Corporation refused to take over the Balmandirs, and the State government supported this decision. The petitioners argued that this violated the constitutional duty to provide pre-primary education. The Gujarat High Court is considering whether the Corporation and State are obligated to continue the Balmandirs and employ the teachers.
legal_issue: Does Article 45 of the Constitution of India, read with the Bombay Provincial Municipal Corporations Act, 1949, and the Gujarat Panchayats Act, 1963, impose a mandatory duty on a Municipal Corporation and the State government to provide and maintain pre-primary education facilities, including the employment of existing teachers, in areas newly merged into the Corporation's jurisdiction?
petitioner_stand: The petitioner argued that Article 45 of the Constitution mandates the State and its instrumentalities, including the Municipal Corporation, to provide free education up to age 14, including pre-primary education. They sought a writ of mandamus directing the respondents to continue the Balmandirs, employ the teachers, and treat them as Corporation employees, arguing that the merger transferred the liability for pre-primary education to the Corporation.
respondent_stand: The Municipal Corporation argued that Article 45 is a directive principle, not enforceable in court, and that the petition was not a Public Interest Litigation. They contended that the teachers were employed by a separate entity (Ahmedabad District Samaj Kalyan Sangh) and that the Corporation had no liability to absorb them. The State government adopted the Corporation's arguments.

case_title: M/S G.S. Convent School vs State Of U.P. And 3 Others (2019)
case_summary: A group of private schools in Uttar Pradesh filed writ petitions seeking government aid or recognition from the U.P. Basic Shiksha Parishad. The schools argued they met the criteria under Article 21A of the Constitution and the Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009, citing government orders that allegedly waived playground requirements. The State of UP contested this, arguing that the schools must meet mandatory criteria under the Act and its rules. The Allahabad High Court examined whether the government orders conflicted with the constitutional right to education, particularly regarding the necessity of playgrounds.
legal_issue: Does the dilution of infrastructure requirements, specifically the necessity of playgrounds, in government orders for granting aid or recognition to schools, contravene Article 21A of the Constitution of India and the Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009?
petitioner_stand: The petitioner schools argued that they are entitled to government aid or recognition based on Article 21A and the Right to Education Act, as well as government orders that allegedly waive or relax the requirement for playgrounds. They sought a writ of mandamus directing the state to grant them aid or recognition.
respondent_stand: The State of U.P. contended that the schools must meet all mandatory criteria prescribed by the Right to Education Act and its associated rules to be eligible for aid or recognition. They argued that the government orders do not supersede the Act's requirements and that the schools must comply with all provisions.
